title: Does a thing's value come from being rare or from being used?
summary: Argue whether the worth of an object lies in its scarcity or in the use it gets, and what each view costs us.
topic: Whether value comes from rarity or from use
prompt: Write a 450–600-word essay arguing whether a thing's value comes from being rare or from being used. Use at least two concrete examples, acknowledge the strongest point on the side you reject, and use cleft sentences, the passive, and hedged generalisation.
hintStarters: We are taught to equate worth with rarity, yet the things we treasure most…
What gives an object its value is not how few of them exist but…
Granted, scarcity does real work — without it…
The clearest test of where value lives is to ask what we would actually miss if…

hintVocab.meaningVi: tích trữ / cất giữ không dùng
phủ bụi / bị bỏ không dùng
một vật gia bảo / vật truyền đời
bị dùng đến nhẵn / mòn nhẵn vì dùng
một phương tiện để đạt mục đích
khóa cất đi / giữ kín không cho dùng
đã dùng nhiều / sờn cũ vì dùng
giá trị tình cảm / giá trị kỷ niệm
bị thèm muốn / được khao khát
bị hao mòn vì sử dụng

sampleText:
There is a teapot in a glass cabinet in a house I once visited in Kyoto. It is three hundred years old, worth more than the car in the drive, and it has never held tea. A few rooms away, the family used a chipped, ordinary pot every single day. When I asked which one they loved, the answer came without hesitation: the chipped one. The valuable teapot, they said, belonged to the cabinet. The cheap one belonged to them.

That exchange has stayed with me because it cuts straight to a question we usually avoid: does a thing's value come from being rare, or from being used? The marketplace gives a confident answer. Price tracks scarcity. A painting nobody can copy, a watch made in a run of fifty, a first edition that survived the fire — what makes these things expensive is precisely how few of them exist. On this view, value is conferred by rarity, and use is almost beside the point.

Granted, this is not nothing. Scarcity does real work. Without some limit on supply, nothing would carry the weight we attach to a wedding ring or an original manuscript; if everyone could have everything, the very idea of treasuring an object would dissolve. The strongest case for the rare side is that scarcity is what lets an object mean "chosen" rather than "available." That point deserves to be taken seriously, not waved away.

And yet the Kyoto teapot exposes the limit of it. An object that is valued only for being rare has, in an important sense, been removed from life. It is locked away precisely so that it cannot be diminished by use — which means it can no longer do the one thing a teapot is for. What is being protected in that cabinet is not the teapot's purpose but its price. The rarer it becomes, the less it is allowed to be a teapot at all.

What gives an object its deepest value, I would argue, is not how few of them exist but how thoroughly it has been woven into a life. The chipped pot is worn smooth at the handle by ten thousand mornings. That wear is not damage; it is biography. A grandmother's well-used recipe book, soft at the corners and stained where the batter splashed, is worth more to the family than any pristine copy could ever be — and worth almost nothing to anyone else. That gap between the two valuations is the whole point. Use creates a kind of worth the market cannot see, because the market only prices what can be sold, and a life cannot be transferred with the object.

None of this means rarity is an illusion. A rare thing can also be a used and loved thing; the two are not enemies. The mistake is to let one swallow the other — to treat the price tag as the measure and forget that the most valuable objects most of us own would fetch nothing at auction. The truest test of where value lives is simple. Ask what you would grieve if it broke. Rarely is it the thing in the cabinet.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to relocate where value lives
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What makes these things expensive is precisely how few of them exist' và 'What gives an object its deepest value … is not how few of them exist but how thoroughly it has been woven into a life' dùng cleft 'What … is …' để chỉ chính xác nguồn gốc của giá trị, và ở câu thứ hai kết hợp 'not X but Y' để lật ngược kết luận ban đầu.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What makes these things expensive is precisely how few of them exist' and 'What gives an object its deepest value … is not how few of them exist but how thoroughly it has been woven into a life' use what-clefts to pinpoint the source of value, the second pairing it with 'not X but Y' to overturn the earlier conclusion.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to hide the agent and stress the object's fate
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It is locked away precisely so that it cannot be diminished by use' và 'What is being protected in that cabinet is not the teapot's purpose but its price' dùng thể bị động để giấu đi chủ thể (ai khóa, ai bảo vệ) và dồn sự chú ý vào số phận của đồ vật — phù hợp với một lập luận trừu tượng về giá trị.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is locked away precisely so that it cannot be diminished by use' and 'What is being protected in that cabinet is not the teapot's purpose but its price' use the passive to suppress the agent (who locks, who protects) and focus on the object's fate — fitting an abstract argument about value.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một cái ấm trà trong một chiếc tủ kính ở một ngôi nhà tôi từng ghé thăm ở Kyoto. Nó ba trăm năm tuổi, đắt hơn cả chiếc xe đậu ngoài sân, và nó chưa bao giờ đựng trà. Cách đó vài phòng, gia đình ấy dùng một cái ấm sứt mẻ, tầm thường mỗi ngày. Khi tôi hỏi họ yêu cái nào, câu trả lời đến không chút do dự: cái ấm sứt. Cái ấm quý giá, họ nói, thuộc về cái tủ. Cái ấm rẻ tiền thì thuộc về họ.

Cuộc trao đổi ấy cứ ở mãi trong tôi vì nó đi thẳng vào một câu hỏi mà ta thường né tránh: giá trị của một thứ đến từ việc nó hiếm có, hay từ việc nó được sử dụng? Thị trường đưa ra một câu trả lời đầy tự tin. Giá cả bám theo sự khan hiếm. Một bức tranh không ai chép được, một chiếc đồng hồ làm trong loạt năm mươi chiếc, một ấn bản đầu tiên sống sót qua đám cháy — điều khiến những thứ này đắt giá chính xác là việc chúng tồn tại ít ỏi đến mức nào. Theo quan điểm này, giá trị được ban cho bởi sự hiếm có, còn việc sử dụng gần như chẳng liên quan.

Công nhận, đây không phải là chuyện vặt. Sự khan hiếm làm một việc thực sự. Nếu không có giới hạn nào đó về nguồn cung, sẽ chẳng có gì mang sức nặng mà ta gán cho một chiếc nhẫn cưới hay một bản thảo gốc; nếu ai cũng có thể có mọi thứ, thì chính ý niệm trân quý một đồ vật sẽ tan biến. Lập luận mạnh nhất của phe ủng hộ sự hiếm có là sự khan hiếm là thứ khiến một đồ vật mang nghĩa "được chọn" thay vì "có sẵn." Luận điểm đó xứng đáng được xem xét nghiêm túc, chứ không phải bị gạt phăng đi.

Vậy mà cái ấm Kyoto lại phơi bày giới hạn của nó. Một đồ vật chỉ được coi trọng vì hiếm có thì, theo một nghĩa quan trọng, đã bị tách khỏi đời sống. Nó bị khóa cất đi chính là để nó không thể bị hao mòn vì sử dụng — điều đó có nghĩa nó không còn có thể làm được cái việc duy nhất mà một cái ấm dùng để làm. Thứ đang được bảo vệ trong cái tủ ấy không phải là công dụng của cái ấm mà là cái giá của nó. Nó càng trở nên hiếm, nó càng ít được phép là một cái ấm trà chút nào.

Điều mang lại cho một đồ vật giá trị sâu sắc nhất, tôi cho rằng, không phải là chúng tồn tại ít ỏi đến đâu mà là nó đã được dệt vào một cuộc đời thấu đáo đến mức nào. Cái ấm sứt mẻ bị mòn nhẵn ở quai cầm bởi mười nghìn buổi sáng. Sự hao mòn ấy không phải là hư hại; nó là tiểu sử. Một cuốn sách công thức nấu ăn của bà được dùng nhiều, mềm nhũn ở các góc và ố vết nơi bột nhão bắn vào, có giá trị với gia đình hơn bất kỳ bản sao nguyên vẹn nào — và gần như chẳng có giá trị gì với người ngoài. Chính khoảng cách giữa hai cách định giá ấy mới là toàn bộ vấn đề. Việc sử dụng tạo ra một loại giá trị mà thị trường không thể nhìn thấy, bởi thị trường chỉ định giá thứ có thể bán được, và một cuộc đời thì không thể được chuyển giao cùng với đồ vật.

Tất cả những điều này không có nghĩa là sự hiếm có là một ảo tưởng. Một vật hiếm cũng có thể là một vật được dùng và được yêu thương; hai điều đó không phải là kẻ thù của nhau. Sai lầm là để cho cái này nuốt chửng cái kia — coi cái nhãn giá là thước đo và quên rằng những đồ vật quý giá nhất mà phần lớn chúng ta sở hữu sẽ chẳng bán được gì ở phiên đấu giá. Bài kiểm tra chân thực nhất về việc giá trị nằm ở đâu thì rất đơn giản. Hãy tự hỏi bạn sẽ tiếc thương điều gì nếu nó vỡ. Hiếm khi đó là cái thứ nằm trong tủ.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to identify the source of market value?
options: What makes these things expensive is precisely how few of them exist.
How few of them exist makes these expensive things what precisely is.
These things are expensive precisely what makes how few exist of them.
Precisely few of them exist what makes these things expensive is how.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What makes these things expensive is …' is a what-cleft locating the cause of their price in scarcity.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence states the strongest point for the rejected side fairly?
options: Some people like rare stuff but they are honestly just being snobs about it.
The strongest case for the rare side is that scarcity is what lets an object mean "chosen" rather than "available."
On the contrary, rarity has never meant anything at all to anybody ever.
Rare things are kind of nice I guess but who really cares about that anyway.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B presents the opposing view at its strongest, which is the fair B2 move before rebutting it.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option states the rival argument seriously rather than mocking it?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive to focus on what is protected, not who protects it?
options: The family protects the price of the teapot, not its purpose, in the cabinet.
What is being protected in that cabinet is not the teapot's purpose but its price.
Protecting the price is what the cabinet does to the teapot's purpose there.
In the cabinet they are protecting price which the teapot's purpose is not.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What is being protected … is not … but …' uses the passive to centre the thing protected, suppressing the agent.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option avoids naming who protects, using 'is being protected'?

options: To share things generously
To store up and keep things, often without using them
To sell things at a profit
To repair broken objects
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To hoard' is to accumulate and hold on to things rather than use or part with them.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of stockpiling and keeping, not using.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the family in Kyoto, where did the valuable teapot 'belong'?
options: To them
To the cabinet
To a museum
To the marketplace
mcQuestions.explanation: The family said the valuable teapot 'belonged to the cabinet' while the cheap one 'belonged to them.'
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the family's answer in the opening paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option keeps a controlled, argumentative register?
options: Rich people locking up teapots is honestly the dumbest thing I have ever heard of.
That wear is not damage; it is biography.
Old chipped pots are way cooler than fancy ones, that's just obviously true.
Nobody should ever buy expensive stuff because it's a total waste of money, period.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B reframes wear in a precise, evocative phrase without slang or overstatement.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'honestly', 'way cooler', 'total waste' — find the measured reframing.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'That wear is not damage; it is biography'?
options: The pot needs to be repaired urgently
The marks of use record the life lived with the object
Old pots are written about in books
Damage always lowers an object's price
mcQuestions.explanation: The 'wear' is reframed as a record of a life — the mornings spent with the pot — rather than as harm.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of what the worn handle is a record of.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'the …er, the …er' structure to show a relationship?
options: The rarer it becomes, the less it is allowed to be a teapot at all.
Rarer becoming it, less allowed to be a teapot it is at all the.
It becomes rare and a teapot less it is allowed to be at all then.
Allowed less to be a teapot the rarer at all it becomes is it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The rarer it becomes, the less it is allowed …' uses the correlative comparative to link rarity to loss of function.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'The more/less …, the more/less …' pattern.

options: Value that comes from how much others will pay
Value something has in itself, apart from its market price
Value measured only in money
Value that decreases over time
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Intrinsic value' is worth that belongs to a thing in itself, independent of what it would sell for.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Intrinsic' means belonging to the thing's own nature.

mcQuestions.prompt: Why, according to the essay, can the market not see the worth that use creates?
options: Because the market only prices what can be sold, and a life cannot be transferred with the object
Because used objects are always dirty
Because no one wants to buy old things
Because markets do not exist for teapots
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says 'the market only prices what can be sold, and a life cannot be transferred with the object.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the sentence explaining why the gap between valuations exists.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence concedes that rarity and use are compatible?
options: Rare things are always useless and used things are always cheap, full stop.
A rare thing can also be a used and loved thing; the two are not enemies.
You can never have something that is both rare and used at the same time.
Rarity destroys use completely and there is nothing to be done about it.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B avoids a false either/or, conceding that an object can be both rare and well used.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option refuses to treat the two values as mutually exclusive?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to be diminished by use' mean?
options: To become more valuable through use
To be reduced, worn down, or lessened by being used
To be cleaned by regular handling
To be hidden away from use
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Diminished by use' means made smaller or worse through use — the very thing the cabinet prevents.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Diminish' means to reduce; apply it to what use does to a rare object.

mcQuestions.prompt: What test does the essay propose for finding where value lives?
options: Ask how much it would sell for
Ask what you would grieve if it broke
Ask how old the object is
Ask how rare the object is
mcQuestions.explanation: The closing proposes the test: 'Ask what you would grieve if it broke.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final lines of the essay.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a 'what' cleft with 'not X but Y' to overturn the market's answer?
options: What gives an object its deepest value is not how few of them exist but how thoroughly it has been woven into a life.
An object's deepest value gives how few exist but woven into a life is what.
Woven into a life what gives value how few exist not but thoroughly is.
How thoroughly woven it is what gives an object value not how few but.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option A is a what-cleft using 'not X but Y' to replace the scarcity answer with the use answer.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What gives … is not … but …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence hedges the writer's own thesis appropriately?
options: Use is the only source of value and rarity is completely worthless nonsense.
What gives an object its deepest value, I would argue, is not how few of them exist but how thoroughly it has been woven into a life.
Everyone who values rare things is simply wrong about everything always.
There is absolutely no situation in which rarity matters even a little bit.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'I would argue' hedges the thesis, presenting it as a reasoned position rather than an absolute fact.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option signals a claim is the writer's argued view, not an absolute?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Look around the room you are in. Which object would fetch the most money, and which would you most grieve to lose? If they are different objects, what does that difference tell you about where you actually locate value?
